subsystem: A privileged mount-helper method, Helper::temporaryMountDevice, from a Deepin desktop-environment file-management stack (dde-file-manager / deepin gvfs-style Helper). Its role is to create an on-demand mount point for a block device and mount that device there, returning the mount path. It is the code path invoked when the UI/backend needs to auto-mount a removable or on-request device into a per-user runtime directory.
provenance: Identified from the Qt idiom (QString, QStringList, QStandardPaths::RuntimeLocation, QDir::mkpath, qApp->applicationName(), the QString::arg() format chain) and the class-qualified method name Helper::temporaryMountDevice, which matches the Deepin dde-file-manager 'Helper' privileged backend. The dCError() logging macro is the Deepin 'dtk'/deepin category-logging convention. The sample banner '/* sample: primevul_train_2254_fixed */' and the filename train_0267_cwe61.cpp signal a PrimeVul-derived sample whose bug class is CWE-61 (UNIX symbolic link following); the code is reviewed blind on its merits regardless of the 'fixed' tag.
trust_boundary: temporaryMountDevice runs inside a privileged Helper (in this family the Helper backend executes as root to perform mount(2)). Its inputs cross a privilege boundary: 'device' is a block-device path and 'name' is a human-readable device identifier that, in this stack, is derived from device metadata (filesystem/volume label or a client-supplied name over the Helper IPC). Both the volume label and any IPC-supplied name are attacker-influenced: a removable disk's label is written by whoever formatted the medium, and an unprivileged local client can drive the Helper. The constructed mount_point path, the mkpath target, and the eventual mount(2) destination are therefore all controlled by hostile string content. The /run/user/<uid> directory tree and any component of it are locations an unprivileged user can pre-create or replace with a symlink.

what_the_code_does.code:
QString Helper::temporaryMountDevice(const QString &device, const QString &name, bool readonly)
{
what_the_code_does.explanation: This is the entry signature of the helper. It takes three parameters: 'device' (a QString naming the block device to mount, e.g. /dev/sdb1), 'name' (a QString used as a per-device label/subdirectory component of the mount point), and 'readonly' (a bool that selects a read-only mount). It returns a QString that is either the resulting mount-point path or an empty string on failure. Because the method belongs to the privileged Helper class, all three parameters originate outside the trust boundary and must be treated as hostile; in particular 'name' will be embedded directly into a filesystem path below.

It first calls mountPoint(device), a lookup that returns the existing mount point for the device if it is already mounted. If that returns a non-empty string the function short-circuits and returns the existing path, avoiding a duplicate mount. This is an idempotency/fast-path guard: only when the device is not already mounted does execution fall through to the path-construction and mount logic below. Note that the freshness/ownership of the returned existing mount point is trusted implicitly.

It sets up a printf-style Qt format template '%1/.%2/mount/%3' where %1 will be a base runtime directory, %2 the application name (prefixed with a dot to form a hidden directory), and %3 the device 'name'. It then queries QStandardPaths for the RuntimeLocation directories, which on Linux resolve to $XDG_RUNTIME_DIR, typically /run/user/<uid>. tmp_paths holds the candidate base directories; the first one will be substituted into %1.

This performs the three positional substitutions into the template. %1 becomes tmp_paths.first() when RuntimeLocation resolved to something, otherwise the hardcoded fallback "/run/user/0" (root's runtime directory). %2 becomes qApp->applicationName(). %3 becomes the untrusted 'name' parameter verbatim — there is no sanitization, canonicalization, or rejection of path metacharacters. The final mount_point is thus something like /run/user/0/.dde-file-manager/mount/<name>, where <name> is fully attacker-influenced and can contain '/', '..', or absolute-path fragments.

It creates the full directory chain for mount_point with QDir::mkpath (equivalent to mkdir -p). mkpath happily creates any number of intermediate directories and, critically, follows existing symbolic links in the path components rather than treating them as an error. On failure it logs via dCError and returns an empty QString to signal failure. Because this runs with the Helper's elevated privileges, the directory tree is created as root along whatever path the substituted string dictates.

It calls mountDevice(device, mount_point, readonly), which performs the actual privileged mount(2) of 'device' onto the freshly created mount_point, honoring the read-only flag. On failure it logs both the device and the target path and returns an empty QString. This is the security-critical action: a filesystem is mounted, as root, at a path derived from untrusted 'name', after a create-then-mount sequence in a user-writable directory tree.

On success it returns the computed mount_point string to the caller, which will treat it as the authoritative location where 'device' is now mounted. The caller may subsequently enumerate, read, or expose files under that path, so any earlier redirection of the path also redirects those follow-on operations.

what_could_go_wrong.explanation: The untrusted 'name' is concatenated into a filesystem path with no sanitization. If 'name' contains '../' sequences (or a leading '/'), the resulting mount_point escapes the intended /run/user/<uid>/.app/mount/ sandbox — e.g. name = "../../../../etc" collapses the path so that mkpath and the privileged mount(2) operate on an attacker-chosen directory. Since the Helper runs as root, this becomes a path-traversal primitive that lets a caller mount a controlled filesystem over sensitive system directories, or create root-owned directories in arbitrary locations. The implementation must reject 'name' values containing '/', '..', or NUL, and must confine the final canonicalized path to be a strict descendant of the runtime base (CWE-22 / CWE-73).

mkpath follows pre-existing symbolic links in every path component. /run/user/<uid> and its subdirectories are writable by the unprivileged target user, so an attacker can pre-plant a symlink at, for example, /run/user/<uid>/.dde-file-manager/mount/<name> (or an intermediate component) that points at /etc, /root, or any directory. When the privileged Helper then runs mkpath and mountDevice, the mount is performed through the symlink onto the attacker-chosen target — classic UNIX symbolic-link following (CWE-61). The safe contract is to create/traverse the mount tree with symlink-refusing primitives (O_NOFOLLOW / openat2(RESOLVE_NO_SYMLINKS) style, or mkdir on each non-symlink component) and to verify the final path is a real directory the attacker does not control before mounting.

There is a time-of-check/time-of-use gap between mkpath creating the directory (line 14) and mountDevice consuming the same path (line 20). Because the enclosing directory is user-writable, an attacker can, in the window between the two calls, delete the just-created directory and replace it (or an ancestor) with a symlink pointing elsewhere, so that mount(2) lands on a different target than was created. This is a TOCTOU symlink race (CWE-367 combined with CWE-61). The implementation must operate on a stable file descriptor (open the created directory with O_NOFOLLOW/O_DIRECTORY once and mount relative to that fd) rather than re-resolving the string path.

The fallback base "/run/user/0" is hardcoded to root's runtime directory whenever QStandardPaths returns no RuntimeLocation (which happens when XDG_RUNTIME_DIR is unset — an environment fully controllable by an unprivileged caller launching or influencing the process). By clearing XDG_RUNTIME_DIR an attacker can force the predictable /run/user/0 base, then pre-create or symlink components under it. Relying on a caller-controllable environment variable to choose a privileged base path is an input-validation weakness (CWE-20 / CWE-426 untrusted search path); the implementation should derive the base from the authenticated caller's real uid, not from ambient environment or a root fallback.

The fast path returns whatever mountPoint(device) reports without re-validating that the reported path is inside the expected runtime tree or that its ownership is correct. If the mount table can be influenced (e.g. a bind mount or a mount of the same device string performed by the attacker), a poisoned lookup result could be returned to the caller as an authoritative mount point, causing follow-on file operations to read attacker-chosen content. The implementation should confirm the returned mount point matches the expected, confined location before trusting it.

Low severity: the error log emits the full, attacker-influenced mount_point (and on line 21 the device and path) into the system/journal log. If 'name' can carry control characters or newlines this enables log injection/forging of log lines (CWE-117), and it discloses the constructed path layout. Minor, but the value should be sanitized/escaped before logging.

summary: A root-privileged mount helper builds a mount-point path by concatenating an untrusted device 'name' into /run/user/<uid>/.app/mount/<name> with no sanitization, then mkpath-creates and mounts onto it inside a user-writable directory tree. This yields a path-traversal + symlink-following (CWE-61) primitive plus a create-then-mount TOCTOU race, letting a local attacker redirect a privileged mount onto an arbitrary directory. The overall risk is high (local privilege escalation via symlink/traversal), gated by how mountDevice and mkpath actually resolve paths.

reviewed_anchors.anchor: Helper::temporaryMountDevice(const QString &device, const QString &name, bool readonly)
reviewed_anchors.reason: Privileged entry point; 'name' flows unsanitized into a filesystem path used for a root mount.
Untrusted device path is mounted and also feeds mountPoint() lookup; contributes to the fast-path trust issue.
Attacker-influenced label embedded verbatim into the mount path — primary traversal/symlink vector.
Only selects mount read-only flag; no memory-safety or path contract of its own.
Fast-path returns an unvalidated existing mount point that could be poisoned.
Simple idempotency guard; no direct vulnerability beyond the trust of mountPoint().
Path template whose %3 is filled with untrusted 'name'; enables traversal when unsanitized.
Base directory derives from caller-controllable XDG_RUNTIME_DIR; empty-case forces root fallback.
Hardcoded root runtime dir used when RuntimeLocation empty; predictable, user-writable-adjacent base.
Concatenation point where 'name' is injected without validation.
Application-controlled constant, not attacker-supplied under normal operation.
Follows symlinks while creating directories as root — symlink-following vector.
Logs untrusted path/device strings — log-injection / info-disclosure (low).
Performs the privileged mount on the untrusted, possibly symlinked path; TOCTOU with mkpath.
Standard failure signaling; no security contract.
Returns computed path; risk is upstream in how the path was built, not in the return.

findings.title: Symlink following in privileged mkpath/mount of an untrusted path
findings.anchor: QDir::current().mkpath(mount_point) followed by mountDevice(device, mount_point, readonly)
findings.bug_class: UNIX symbolic link following (privileged mount through attacker-planted symlink)
findings.invariant: Every component of mount_point must be created and resolved without following symbolic links (O_NOFOLLOW / RESOLVE_NO_SYMLINKS, or per-component mkdir that refuses existing symlinks), and the final mount target must be a real directory not controllable by the unprivileged user before mount(2) is issued.
findings.failure_mode: mount_point lives under /run/user/<uid>, which the target user can write. The user pre-plants a symlink at an intermediate or final component pointing to /etc, /root, etc. QDir::mkpath follows it and mountDevice performs a root mount onto the attacker-chosen directory, shadowing or exposing privileged content — local privilege escalation / arbitrary root mount.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether mkpath/mountDevice resolve mount_point with symlink protections or naively via the string path; confirm no user-writable component is followed as a symlink.

findings.title: Path traversal via unsanitized device 'name' concatenated into mount path
findings.bug_class: Path traversal / external control of filesystem path
findings.invariant: 'name' must be validated to a safe single path component (no '/', no '..', no leading path separator, no NUL) before being interpolated into a path that a privileged operation acts on.
findings.failure_mode: An attacker who controls the device label / IPC 'name' supplies '../../../../<target>'; the .arg(name) substitution produces a mount_point that resolves outside /run/user/<uid>/.app/mount/, so the root Helper mkpath-creates and mounts at an arbitrary location, escaping the intended sandbox.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether 'name' is filtered upstream; test with name values containing '../' and '/' to see if mount_point escapes the runtime directory.

findings.title: Create-then-mount TOCTOU race on a user-writable directory
findings.bug_class: TOCTOU symlink race between directory creation and mount
findings.invariant: The directory created by mkpath and the directory mounted onto must be provably the same inode; achieve this by opening the created dir with O_DIRECTORY|O_NOFOLLOW and mounting relative to that fd, rather than re-resolving the path string.
findings.failure_mode: In the window after mkpath and before mountDevice, the unprivileged user swaps the created directory (or an ancestor) for a symlink; the subsequent path-based mount resolves to a different, attacker-chosen target.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether mountDevice re-resolves the string path (racy) or uses a fd captured at creation time.

findings.title: Caller-controllable base path with hardcoded root-runtime fallback
findings.bug_class: Reliance on untrusted environment for a privileged search path
findings.invariant: The base runtime directory for a privileged mount must be derived from the authenticated caller's real uid, not from ambient XDG_RUNTIME_DIR nor a hardcoded /run/user/0 fallback.
findings.failure_mode: By unsetting XDG_RUNTIME_DIR the caller forces RuntimeLocation empty, selecting the predictable /run/user/0 base; combined with the traversal/symlink issues this gives the attacker a known, targetable base directory for planting symlinks.
findings.what_to_confirm: How XDG_RUNTIME_DIR is populated for the privileged Helper process and whether /run/user/0 is ever reachable in practice.

findings.title: Unvalidated existing mount point returned from fast path
findings.anchor: QString mount_point = mountPoint(device); if (!mount_point.isEmpty()) return mount_point;
findings.invariant: A mount point reported by mountPoint(device) must be confirmed to reside within the expected confined runtime tree and be owned appropriately before it is returned as authoritative.
findings.failure_mode: If the mount table can be influenced (bind mount / matching device string), mountPoint returns an attacker-chosen path that the caller then trusts for follow-on file access.
findings.what_to_confirm: What mountPoint() matches on and whether its result is location/ownership-validated before use.

findings.title: Untrusted path/device strings written to logs
findings.invariant: Attacker-controlled strings (name-derived mount_point, device) must be escaped/sanitized before being emitted to the system log.
findings.failure_mode: A 'name' or 'device' containing newlines/control characters forges or splits log lines, and the log discloses the internal path layout.
findings.what_to_confirm: Whether dCError escapes control characters in the %s arguments.

audit_checklist: Confirm whether 'name' is validated to a single safe path component (reject '/', '..', leading separators, NUL) anywhere before line 12; test with '../' payloads.
Verify mkpath and mountDevice resolve mount_point without following symlinks (O_NOFOLLOW / RESOLVE_NO_SYMLINKS or per-component non-symlink mkdir).
Check that mountDevice mounts relative to a fd captured at creation, not by re-resolving the string path, to close the mkpath->mount TOCTOU window.
Determine the real uid/ownership of /run/user/<uid> for the privileged Helper and whether the /run/user/0 fallback is ever reachable via unset XDG_RUNTIME_DIR.
Confirm mountPoint(device) results are validated for location and ownership before being returned by the fast path.
Confirm dCError escapes control characters in attacker-controlled %s arguments before logging.
Verify the privilege level mountDevice actually runs at and which clients/labels can reach temporaryMountDevice across the IPC boundary.
